ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic custard entry (FOODON_03301116). No specific entry exists for Phoenix Milk Custard with rice wine and mochi. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Milk-based desserts) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for this Cantonese stir-fried milk custard preparation with rice wine and mochi.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Cantonese dessert preparation.

allergens: gluten (mochi may contain trace gluten depending on preparation)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard dessert bowl of Phoenix Milk Custard with Rice Wine and Mochi, approximately 200 g, as typically served in a Cantonese dessert restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Custard, egg-based, prepared from recipe (USDA SR Legacy #19270) and Mochi, rice cake (USDA FoodData Central #168882), combined with whole milk and fermented rice wine component estimates
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Phoenix Milk Custard with Rice Wine and Mochi exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 55% milk custard base (egg yolk, whole milk, sugar, cornstarch) and 45% glutinous rice mochi, with a small contribution from osmanthus fermented rice wine (~5% by weight). Reference anchors: USDA SR Legacy custard egg-based (#19270), USDA FoodData Central glutinous rice mochi (#168882), and USDA whole milk (#1097). Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of glutinous rice and milk-based desserts — analogous estimate based on: (1) Glutinous rice GI ~98 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) and (2) Egg custard GI ~35 (Foster-Powell K et al., 2002, Am J Clin Nutr); weighted composite estimate for this mixed dessert
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Phoenix Milk Custard with Rice Wine and Mochi. GI estimated as weighted composite: mochi component (glutinous rice GI ~98, weight ~45%) and custard base (egg custard GI ~35, weight ~55%), yielding an estimated blended GI of approximately 55 (low). Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (55 × 29.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 32.4 g. This is an analogous estimate; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (~55 mg per 100 g; ~110 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: The sodium content of this dessert is relatively low at approximately 110 mg per serving, making it acceptable for individuals with hypertension when consumed occasionally, though portion control remains advisable given the sugar and caloric load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~32 g per serving) driven by glutinous rice mochi and added sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dessert due to its high glycemic load of approximately 32 g per 200 g serving, primarily attributable to the glutinous rice mochi (GI ~98) and added sugar; portion reduction or substitution with lower-GI alternatives is strongly recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from egg yolk and whole milk (~2.9 g per 100 g) and moderate cholesterol (~95 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (~5.8 g per serving) and cholesterol (~190 mg per serving) from egg yolk and whole milk warrant moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease, particularly those managing LDL cholesterol levels; occasional consumption as part of a balanced diet is preferable to regular intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Phoenix Milk Custard with Rice Wine and Mochi is a traditional Cantonese dessert originating from Guangdong, China, and popular in Singapore's Cantonese dessert restaurants (糖水铺). The dish is prepared by stir-frying a silky custard base made from fresh whole milk, egg yolk, sugar, and cornstarch until it achieves a smooth, creamy, and slightly firm texture. The custard is then enriched with osmanthus-infused fermented glutinous rice wine (酒酿), which imparts a delicate floral aroma and a subtle sweetness with mild alcoholic notes. Soft, chewy glutinous rice mochi pieces are added to provide textural contrast. The dessert is served warm or at room temperature. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (4.8 g) from milk and egg, moderate fat (5.2 g) including saturated fat from dairy and egg yolk, and a significant carbohydrate load (29.5 g) primarily from mochi and added sugar. The glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is high (~32 g), making it a concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan or halal due to the fermented rice wine content. It is a culturally significant dessert in Cantonese cuisine, often enjoyed as an evening sweet soup course.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Phoenix Milk Custard with Rice Wine and Mochi was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA SR Legacy and FoodData Central reference entries for egg custard, glutinous rice mochi, and whole milk. GI is an analogous composite estimate. Confidence is medium.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from 'Beverages' (as in input) to 'Desserts', which accurately reflects the nature of this Cantonese milk custard dessert. 2. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component references (custard egg-based SR Legacy #19270, glutinous rice mochi #168882, whole milk #1097) using a weighted composition model (~55% custard base, ~45% mochi, ~5% rice wine). Direct laboratory analysis is recommended for production-quality records. 3. GI is an analogous composite estimate (glutinous rice GI ~98 per Atkinson et al. 2008; egg custard GI ~35 per Foster-Powell et al. 2002); direct GI measurement is recommended. The GI of 55 is classified as 'low'. 4. The dish is not halal due to the presence of fermented rice wine (alcohol-containing ingredient). 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA reference data for this food type; no industrial trans fats are present in the custard or mochi components. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g (1.6), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.4) are estimated from USDA whole milk and egg yolk fatty acid profiles. 7. All -1 values in per_100g represent nutrients not individually reported in the USDA component references used for this estimation; specifically: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are estimated (not -1), all other micronutrients are estimated from component references and reported as positive values. No -1 sentinel values remain in this record. 8. Serving size of 200 g is estimated based on typical Cantonese dessert restaurant portion sizes in Singapore.
